15.1.title: The inverted-tree image — grammar matches metaphor
15.1.body: ऊर्ध्व-मूलम् अधः-शाखम् अश्वत्थम् = "the aśvattha with-root-above with-branches-below." Two बहुव्रीहि compounds in द्वितीया, both with direction-words flipped: root UP, branches DOWN. Sanskrit's compound-direction grammar carries the cosmic inversion. The tree-image is the saṃsāra-tree (cosmic banyan) rooted in Brahman, branching down into manifestation.

15.1.title: तं वेद स वेद-वित् — the conditional knower
15.1.body: Relative-correlative chain: यस्य ... यः तं वेद स वेद-वित् = "of which the chants are leaves; he who knows that, he is the Veda-knower." वेद is लट् 3sg of √विद् ("knows"); वेद-वित् is उपपद तत्पुरुष ("Veda-knower"). The verse turns Veda-knowledge into the precondition for understanding the cosmic tree.

15.2.title: Six modifiers of the tree's branches
15.2.body: अधश्च ऊर्ध्वं प्रसृताः ("spread below and above") + तस्य शाखा ("its branches") + गुण-प्रवृद्धाः ("grown by guṇas") + विषय-प्रवालाः ("with sense-objects as shoots") + अधश्च मूलानि अनुसन्ततानि ("and roots-stretched-down") + कर्म-अनुबन्धीनि मनुष्य-लोके ("action-binding in the human world"). Six modifiers stacked on शाखा — the verse is one long enumeration of the tree's morphology.

15.2.title: विषय-प्रवालाः — sense-objects as shoots
15.2.body: बहुव्रीहि: "those whose shoots are sense-objects." प्रवाल = the tender new shoot, the leaf-bud. Krishna picks the most ALIVE part of the tree (the growing tip) and identifies it with the senses. The image is grammatically a possessive compound describing the branches: it's their bud-stage that is sense-experience.

15.3.title: Three negations + the act of cutting
15.3.body: न रूपम् अस्य ... न अन्तः न च आदिः न च सम्प्रतिष्ठा = "its form is not [grasped], no end, no beginning, no foundation." Then the imperative-via-absolutive: छित्त्वा ("having cut"). The negation-cascade names the tree's untraceability, and the resolution is to CUT, not to understand.

15.3.title: सुविरूढ-मूलम् + असङ्ग-शस्त्रेण दृढेन — the surgical tools
15.3.body: Object: "the aśvattha with deeply-grown root" (PPP कर्मधारय compound + द्वितीया). Instrument: "with the firm sword of non-attachment" (तृतीया तृतीया-तत्पुरुष + adjective दृढेन). Sanskrit's case-grammar names the surgical setup: deeply-rooted tree → cut → with non-attachment as weapon.

15.4.title: तत्-परिमार्गितव्यम् — the gerundive of seeking
15.4.body: ततः पदं तत् परिमार्गितव्यम् = "then that station should be sought out." परिमार्गितव्यम् is गेरुन्डिवे / यत्-pratyaya (verbal adjective of obligation): "[that] which is to-be-sought." तत् is the matched correlative. After the cutting (15.3 absolutive), the seeking is obligatory.

15.4.title: Long relative-correlative spanning the verse
15.4.body: यस्मिन् गताः न निवर्तन्ति ("in which gone, [they] do not return") + तम् एव चाद्यं पुरुषं प्रपद्ये ("I take refuge in that primordial puruṣa") + यतः प्रवृत्तिः प्रसृता पुराणी ("from whom the ancient activity has flowed"). Three relative-clauses, three different relative pronouns (यस्मिन्, यतः, [implicit which-puruṣa]). Sanskrit's correlative grammar packs the whole cosmology.

15.5.title: Bahuvrīhi cascade of the seeker
15.5.body: निर्-मान-मोहाः ("those whose pride-and-delusion are gone"), जित-सङ्ग-दोषाः ("those who have conquered the defects of attachment"), अध्यात्म-नित्याः ("perpetually [oriented] toward the inner self"), विनिवृत्त-कामाः ("those whose desires have receded"). Four parallel बहुव्रीहि nominatives — all describing the same plural subject ("they").

15.5.title: द्वंद्वैः विमुक्ताः — d̥vandva = "dualities"
15.5.body: तृतीया द्वंद्वैः सुख-दुःख-संज्ञैः = "by dualities named-pleasure-and-pain." तृतीया-of-separation. The grammatical द्वंद्व compound that names "pleasure-pain" is also a literal philosophical dvandva (= duality). Sanskrit's grammar terminology IS its philosophy here — द्वंद्व the compound type is named after the existential dualities that bind.

15.6.title: Three subjects, one negation
15.6.body: न तत् भासयते सूर्यः न शशाङ्कः न पावकः = "sun does not illuminate that, nor moon, nor fire." Three nominatives parallel; one passive verb भासयते ("illuminates," लट् 3sg). The verse argues that the supreme abode is itself the source of light — not lit BY anything.

15.6.title: तत् धाम परमं मम — the ablative-of-no-return
15.6.body: यद् गत्वा न निवर्तन्ते = "having gone to which, they do not return." गत्वा is absolutive of √गम्; न निवर्तन्ते is लट् 3pl. Then: तत् धाम परमं मम ("that supreme abode is mine"). The verse's pivot from "not illuminated by" to "my abode" is grammatically a series of तत्-clauses anchored on the शिविरम्.

15.7.title: मम एव अंशः — the partial-incarnation claim
15.7.body: मम एव अंशः जीव-लोके जीव-भूतः = "my own portion in the world-of-jīvas, having-become-the-jīva." मम (षष्ठी) + अंशः (predicate noun) + locative जीव-लोके + present participle जीव-भूतः ("having-become-living"). Sanskrit packs "Iśvara's partial manifestation as jīva" into a tight chain of cases.

15.7.title: मनः-षष्ठानि इन्द्रियाणि — six-with-mind
15.7.body: मनः-षष्ठानि (बहुव्रीहि: "those of whom mind is the sixth") + इन्द्रियाणि (the senses). The five senses + mind = the six-fold sensory apparatus. प्रकृति-स्थानि ("established in prakṛti") + कर्षति ("drags," लट् 3sg of √कृष्). The jīva drags the six-fold apparatus through bodies.

15.8.title: Two relative clauses naming bodies
15.8.body: शरीरं यद् अवाप्नोति यच्च अपि उत्क्रामति = "the body which [the īśvara] obtains and which it transcends." Two यद् clauses on bodies — entering and leaving. Then the verb: एतानि गृहीत्वा संयाति ("having taken these, it transmigrates").

15.8.title: वायुः गन्धान् इव आशयात् — the wind-and-scent simile
15.8.body: तृतीया वायुः (subject of similae), द्वितीया गन्धान् ("scents," object), पञ्चमी आशयात् ("from a vessel"). "As wind [carries] scents FROM a vessel." The simile encodes transmigration: scents are non-physical-yet-transportable, like the jīva's mental-tendencies. Single locative would have been weaker; पञ्चमी emphasises the FROM-ness.

15.9.title: Five senses listed, then absolutive + verb
15.9.body: श्रोत्रं चक्षुः स्पर्शनं रसनं घ्राणम् — ear, eye, touch, taste, smell. Five द्वितीया objects of the absolutive अधिष्ठाय ("having taken-station-over"). Plus मनः ("mind"). Then the main verb उपसेवते ("resorts-to," लट् 3sg ātmanepada).

15.9.title: विषयान् उपसेवते — "resorts to sense-objects"
15.9.body: उप + सेवते = "approaches-and-serves" / "resorts to." The verb is ātmanepada — the indriya-controller serves the objects FOR HIMSELF. Reflexive emphasis: the jīva uses senses on its own behalf. Then 15.10 says fools can't even SEE this happening.

15.10.title: Three participles, three states, then the negation
15.10.body: उत्क्रामन्तम् ("leaving," शतृ present participle), स्थितम् ("settled," PPP), वा अपि भुञ्जानम् ("or even enjoying," present middle participle). Three different states of the jīva, all in द्वितीया as objects of the negated verb न अनुपश्यन्ति ("do not see"). The fools (विमूढाः) fail to see in any of these states.

15.10.title: ज्ञान-चक्षुषः — "those with the eye of knowledge"
15.10.body: बहुव्रीहि nominative plural: "those whose eye is knowledge" / "those-with-knowledge-as-eye." Contrasts with विमूढाः ("the deluded"). The verse's structure is binary: fools-don't-see / wise-do-see. Same verb पश्यन्ति ("see"), opposite outcomes.

15.11.title: Same word, two yields
15.11.body: यतन्तः योगिनः अपि एनम् पश्यन्ति ("the striving yogis also see this") + यतन्तः अपि अकृत-आत्मानः न एनम् पश्यन्ति अचेतसः ("the striving ones who are not self-perfected, the unconscious, do not see"). The present participle यतन्तः ("striving") appears in BOTH halves — striving alone is not enough.

15.11.title: अकृत-आत्मानः + अचेतसः — the double diagnosis
15.11.body: Two बहुव्रीहि-नञ् + मतुप् compound: "those whose self is unmade" / "those without consciousness." The unconscious-striving-one is grammatically constructed as a negation-cascade. Krishna doesn't fault them for not striving; he names what's missing inside the striving.

15.12.title: Three relative clauses, one assertion
15.12.body: यद् आदित्य-गतम् तेजः ... यद् चन्द्रमसि यद् च अग्नौ — three locative-based relative clauses (in sun / in moon / in fire). Each is a यद् clause modifying the implicit "that light which." Then: तत् तेजः विद्धि मामकम् ("know that light to be mine"). The grammatical strategy: distribute the claim across the three lights, then collect them into one तत्.

15.12.title: Locative as housing
15.12.body: आदित्य-गतम् ("sun-gone"), चन्द्रमसि ("in the moon"), अग्नौ ("in fire") — three different ways of saying "in." Compound first, then explicit सप्तमी twice. Sanskrit gives multiple grammatical channels for the same semantic relation, depending on metrical and stylistic constraints.

15.13.title: Two parallel actions of Iśvara
15.13.body: (a) गाम् आविश्य भूतानि धारयामि अहम् ओजसा = "having entered the earth, I support beings with vital force." (b) सोमः भूत्वा रसात्मकः पुष्णामि च औषधीः सर्वाः = "having become Soma, juice-natured, I nourish all plants." Two absolutives (आविश्य, भूत्वा), two finite verbs (धारयामि, पुष्णामि). The pattern is symmetric: enter-X → do-Y.

15.13.title: रसात्मकः — "juice-natured"
15.13.body: बहुव्रीहि: "of whom the self is juice." The Moon (सोम) is identified with rasa — sap, savor, the moisture that makes plants grow. Krishna's claim: the cosmic juice-conduit IS me. Grammatically, the बहुव्रीहि compresses the entire mythopoetic identification.

15.14.title: वैश्वानरः — the digestive fire-self
15.14.body: अहं वैश्वानरः भूत्वा प्राणिनाम् देहम् आश्रितः = "I, having become Vaiśvānara, having entered the body of living-beings." Two absolutives describe the residence-act. वैश्वानर is the universal digestive fire residing in the belly. Krishna identifies as the metabolizer-of-food in every body.

15.14.title: Four-fold food classification
15.14.body: पचामि अन्नं चतुर्-विधम् = "I cook the four-fold food" (चतुर्विध = the द्विगु compound for the four eating-modes: chewed, sucked, licked, drunk). The verb पचामि is the body's metabolism, divine-positioned. Sanskrit's compound numeralization encodes the entire dietary taxonomy in one adjective.

15.15.title: Three ablatives of mental origination
15.15.body: मत्तः स्मृतिः ज्ञानम् अपोहनं च = "from me [come] memory, knowledge, and forgetting." मत्तः is the ablative of "I" — पञ्चमी अहम् = मत्तः. Three nominative subjects, one implicit source-verb. The verse claims THREE mental functions (including forgetting) all come from Iśvara.

15.15.title: Three identifications via emphatic एव
15.15.body: वेदैः सर्वैः अहम् एव वेद्यः = "by all the Vedas, I alone am to-be-known" (तृतीया-of-agent in the passive). वेदान्त-कृत् ("the maker of Vedānta") + वेद-वित् एव च अहम् ("and I alone am the Veda-knower"). The एव's seal it: not partially, not among-others, but exclusively.

15.16.title: द्वौ — the dual that frames the chapter's punchline
15.16.body: द्वौ इमौ पुरुषौ लोके — dual nominative: "these two puruṣas in the world." Then their names: क्षरः च अक्षरः एव च ("the perishable and the imperishable"). क्षरः = सर्वाणि भूतानि ("all beings"). कूट-स्थः अक्षरः उच्यते ("the immovable is called akṣara"). The chapter has been building toward THIS dichotomy; verse 16 names it.

15.16.title: उच्यते — the passive of nomenclature
15.16.body: लट् 3sg passive of √वच्. Used here in its specific Sanskrit function: "is called X." Krishna pulls back from first-person claims and gives the philosophical tradition's vocabulary: kṣara, akṣara. The shift to nominal-passive marks a step out of polemic into shared terminology.

15.17.title: उत्तमः पुरुषः अन्यः — the third one
15.17.body: After 15.16 names two (kṣara, akṣara), 15.17 introduces a THIRD: "there is another, the highest puruṣa, called paramātmā" — परमात्मा इति उदाहृतः ("named paramātmā," PPP passive). The mark of the dichotomy is to be transcended. Krishna deploys the rhetorical move of "two and then surprise — three."

15.17.title: Three modifiers of the highest puruṣa
15.17.body: यः लोक-त्रयम् आविश्य बिभर्ति अव्ययः ईश्वरः = "who, having entered the three worlds, sustains [them] — the unchanging Iśvara." Absolutive आविश्य, finite बिभर्ति (लट् 3sg of √भृ), two nominative epithets अव्ययः and ईश्वरः. Sanskrit packs the cosmic role-description into one tight relative clause.

15.18.title: "Therefore I am" — Krishna's self-naming
15.18.body: यस्मात् क्षरम् अतीतः अहम् अक्षरात् अपि च उत्तमः = "because I have passed beyond the perishable and am higher even than the imperishable." Two ablatives (क्षरम् as object of अतीत; अक्षरात् पञ्चमी governing the comparative उत्तम). The conclusion: अतः लोके वेदे च प्रथितः पुरुषोत्तमः ("I am celebrated in world and Veda as Puruṣottama"). The chapter's title-naming.

15.18.title: Comparative + ablative + comparative
15.18.body: अक्षरात् अपि उत्तमः — "higher EVEN than the imperishable." The अपि is critical: it concedes that akṣara is high, then says ego (Puruṣottama) is HIGHER. The pedagogical structure: build up akṣara as the Vedic absolute, then place Puruṣottama above it.

15.19.title: असम्मूढः — the precondition for knowing
15.19.body: यः माम् एवम् असम्मूढः जानाति पुरुषोत्तमम् = "who thus, unbewildered, knows me as Puruṣottama." असम्मूढः (बहुव्रीहि-नञ् PPP: "one whose bewilderment is gone") is the modifier of the knower. Krishna is precise: it isn't knowledge that frees from delusion — UN-delusion is the precondition for the knowledge.

15.19.title: सर्व-भावेन — "with whole being"
15.19.body: तृतीया: "by/with the whole-being." सर्व-भाव compound covers heart, mind, intention, action. Krishna names the totality required for worship. Then मां भजति ("worships me") + सर्व-वित् ("all-knower," उपपद तत्पुरुष): the worshipper becomes the all-knower.

15.20.title: गुह्य-तम — the superlative of secrecy
15.20.body: इति गुह्य-तमं शास्त्रम् इदम् उक्तम् मया = "this most-secret scripture has thus been spoken by me." गुह्य-तम is superlative of गुह्य ("hidden"). The chapter's closing self-evaluation: not just secret, but MOST-secret. मया is तृतीया-agent of PPP उक्तम्.

15.20.title: बुद्धि-मान् + कृत-कृत्यः — chapter-closing predicates
15.20.body: एतद् बुद्ध्वा बुद्धिमान् स्यात् कृत-कृत्यः च = "having understood this, one would become wise and accomplished." बुद्धिमान् is मतुप् stem; कृत-कृत्यः is बहुव्रीहि ("one whose to-be-done has been done"). विधिलिङ् स्यात् ("would be"). The chapter ends with the conditional outcome of the teaching's reception.
